description: The provided image is a non-scientific graphical user interface placeholder, featuring a logo with a red bookmark icon inside a partial blue and yellow circular ring, below which the text 'Check for updates' is printed. No content relevant to the associated research on Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C high Al added low-density medium Mn steel, including material micrographs, characterization spectra, mechanical performance plots, processing flow charts, or other research-related visuals, is identifiable in this asset. No scientific conclusions from the corresponding study can be verified visually from this placeholder image.

This figure consists of three subplots for the high Al (~5 wt.%) added Fe-8Mn-5Al-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel. Subplot (a) shows the schematic thermomechanical processing route after casting: homogenization at 1100 °C for 2 h, hot forging followed by air cooling, hot rolling with 1100 °C soaking for 2 h, intermediate reheating, water quenching, and final intercritical annealing at 750 °C for 5-180 min with subsequent air cooling. Subplot (b) presents Thermo-Calc calculated equilibrium phase volume fractions versus temperature, with BCC_A2 (ferrite) and FCC_A1 (austenite) as major phases, and a dual-phase region of ~650-1350 °C, supporting the selection of 750 °C as intercritical annealing temperature. Subplot (c) displays dilatometry curves of hot forged samples with 5 min and 15 min holding time, showing only minor deviation around ~600 °C, indicating no massive ferrite-to-austenite transformation during heating and verifying the high delta ferrite stability predicted by thermodynamic calculation. This figure provides fundamental support for processing design and phase transformation analysis of the studied steel.

This figure characterizes the phase composition and microstructure of as-cast and hot forged high-Al (~5 wt%) low-density medium Mn steel (Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C). Subplot (a) presents XRD patterns (2θ 40–120°) for cast (black line) and hot forged (red line) specimens, confirming both contain BCC δ-ferrite and FCC austenite; Rietveld refinement yields ~26 vol% austenite in cast samples and ~29 vol% in forged samples. Subplots (b,c) are optical micrographs of cast specimens, showing dendritic austenite (marked by blue arrows) in a δ-ferrite matrix. Subplots (d,e) are optical micrographs of hot forged specimens, with residual dendritic austenite (blue arrow) remaining due to insufficient pre-forging soaking. Subplot (f) is an SEM micrograph of the forged specimen showing banded δ/γ morphology, while subplot (g) is an EBSD phase map (red: BCC ferrite, green: FCC austenite) validating phase distribution. The figure demonstrates hot forging marginally increases austenite fraction while partially dissolving cast dendritic microstructure, supporting that elemental partitioning during soaking improves austenite stability.

This figure characterizes the microstructure, phase composition and elemental partitioning of hot rolled & quenched (HRQ) Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C low-density medium Mn steel. Subfigure (a) is an optical microscopy image with inserted differential interference contrast (DIC) image, 100 μm scale bar, showing elongated δ-ferrite (BCC) and α'/γ (martensite/austenite) phases aligned along the rolling direction. Subfigure (b) is a scanning electron microscopy image with 10 μm scale bar, presenting the fine morphology of δ-ferrite and lenticular martensite transformed from austenite during water quenching. Subfigure (c) shows EDS analysis results: δ-ferrite contains 5.61±0.11 wt% Al and 7.02±0.25 wt% Mn, while austenite/martensite contains 4.75±0.11 wt% Al and 8.36±0.16 wt% Mn, confirming Al enrichment in δ-ferrite and Mn enrichment in FCC phases. Subfigure (d) is an EBSD phase map superimposed on IQ map with 100 μm scale bar, red for BCC phases and green for FCC phases, verifying the phase distribution. The figure confirms partial austenite transforms to martensite during quenching, while δ-ferrite remains stable as the matrix phase.

This figure presents scanning electron microscopy (SEM) micrographs and corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis of high-Al (~5 wt%) low-density Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C medium Mn steel samples intercritically annealed at 750 °C for 5 min (A1–A3), 30 min (B1–B3), 60 min (C1–C3), 120 min (D1–D3), and 180 min (E1–E3). Subfigures *1 are low-magnification SEM showing overall microstructures with stable δ-ferrite matrix and ά/γ (tempered martensite/austenite) regions; *2 are high-magnification SEM revealing reverted austenite morphology (needle-shaped at short annealing times, evolving to mixed needle-globular at 60 min, dissolving at longer durations); *3 are EDS spectra and composition tables quantifying Al and Mn weight percentages in δ-ferrite, untransformed austenite, reverted austenite, and intercritical ferrite. Key findings include invariant δ-ferrite composition across all annealing durations confirming its high stability, Mn-leaner reverted austenite relative to untransformed austenite, and gradual dissolution of reverted austenite into intercritical ferrite with increasing annealing time, with the 60 min sample showing optimum reverted austenite morphology for enhanced TRIP effect. This figure validates phase transformation and elemental partitioning mechanisms during intercritical annealing, linking microstructure evolution to austenite stability.

This figure presents 9 subfigures each containing nanoindentation load-displacement curves (y-axis: Load in mN, x-axis: Displacement in nm) paired with inset optical micrographs of indent impressions on specific phases of high (~5 wt%) Al added low-density medium Mn steel. Subfigures A1-A3 correspond to hot rolled & quenched (HRQ) samples, B1-B3 to samples intercritically annealed at 750 °C for 5 min, C1-C3 to samples intercritically annealed at 750 °C for 60 min. For each sample group, subfigures with suffix 1 test δ-ferrite, suffix 2 test martensite/reversed austenite, suffix 3 test untransformed austenite, all at 30 mN maximum load. Key observations: martensite exhibits the smallest penetration depth (~384 nm, highest hardness ~8897±550 MPa), followed by δ-ferrite (hardness ~4439±206 MPa for HRQ, negligible change with annealing), then untransformed austenite (~550 nm penetration, ~4080±360 MPa for HRQ, no significant change with annealing), while reversed austenite has the lowest hardness (3867±110 MPa for 5 min anneal, 3478±334 MPa for 60 min anneal, decreasing with longer annealing time). This figure confirms phase-dependent hardness trends, validates that hard martensite dissolves into softer reversed austenite during intercritical annealing, and shows δ-ferrite and untransformed austenite hardness are insensitive to the tested annealing durations.

This group of figures presents mechanical properties of high (~5%) Al added low-density medium Mn steel. Subplot A1 shows engineering stress-strain curves, A2 shows true stress-strain curves for hot rolled (HR) and intercritically annealed at 750 °C specimens (IA750_5 Min, IA750_30 Min, IA750_60 Min, IA750_120 Min, IA750_180 Min). Subplots B1-B4 display work hardening rate curves for IA750_5 Min, IA750_30 Min, IA750_60 Min, IA750_180 Min specimens respectively, each with two deformation stages: S1 (sharp decrease of work hardening rate, attributed to δ-ferrite deformation) and S2 (slow linear decrease of work hardening rate, from concurrent softening and TRIP effect). The HR sample has the highest strength but poorest ductility. As annealing time increases from 5 min to 60 min, ductility and mean work hardening rate in S2 increase, with IA750_60 Min showing the highest S2 work hardening rate (1000-2000 MPa), optimum austenite stability and strongest TRIP effect leading to the best strength-ductility combination. Further extending annealing time to 180 min reduces work hardening rate and ductility. This figure supports that 750 °C intercritical annealing for 60 min yields optimal mechanical performance for the tested steel.

This four-subplot figure characterizes high ~5 wt% Al added low-density Fe-7.79Mn-4.88Al-0.84Si-0.2C medium Mn steel processed via hot rolling quenching (HRQ) and intercritical annealing at 750 °C for 5, 30, 60, 120, 180 min (IA750_*Min samples). Subplots (a) and (b) are XRD patterns collected before and after tensile testing respectively, identifying α-ferrite (BCC) and γ-austenite (FCC) phases with indexed diffraction peaks; post-tensile XRD shows reduced austenite peak intensity from strain-induced martensitic (TRIP) transformation. Subplot (c) quantifies pre/post-tensile austenite volume fraction, austenite transformation ratio, and mechanical stability (K): austenite fraction peaks at 46% for IA750_5Min, while transformation ratio peaks at 20% for IA750_60Min, indicating optimal TRIP effect at 60 min annealing. Subplot (d) presents tensile properties: yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), total elongation (TE), and product of strength and elongation (PSE), with IA750_60Min achieving the highest PSE (~7.9 GPa%) and TE (~12%). The core conclusion is that 60 min intercritical annealing at 750 °C delivers the optimal strength-ductility combination for the studied steel.

This schematic illustrates the phase transformation evolution of high ~5 wt.% Al added low-density medium Mn steel during intercritical annealing at 750 °C for varied durations. Subfigure (a) shows the hot rolled & quenched (HRQ) specimen, which consists of δ ferrite matrix, untransformed austenite (γ⁰) and martensite (α'). Subfigures (b) to (f) correspond to samples intercritically annealed for 5 min, 30 min, 60 min, 120 min, 180 min, respectively. With increasing annealing time, martensite gradually transforms into needle-shaped reverted austenite (γᵣₑᵥ) and intercritical ferrite (α) at the early stage, then reverted austenite morphology changes to globular shape, its fraction decreases as austenite stabilizing elements (Mn, C) partition to adjacent untransformed austenite, leading to reduced stability of reverted austenite. The sample annealed for 60 min achieves balanced size and composition of reverted austenite, delivering optimum austenite stability and enhanced TRIP effect, while 180 min annealing results in nearly full dissolution of reverted austenite and negligible TRIP effect.

This set of figures presents HRTEM and EDS characterization results of the IA750_60 Min specimen, a high (~5 wt.%) Al added low-density medium Mn steel intercritically annealed at 750 °C for 60 min. Subfigure (a) is the bright-field TEM image, (b) is the high-angle annular dark-field (HAADF) image, (c) to (g) show EDS elemental mappings of Fe, Mn, Al, Si, and C respectively, and (h) is the overlaid EDS mapping of all elements on the HAADF image, with a uniform scale bar of 500 nm. EDS results confirm that reverted austenite is enriched with Mn and depleted of Al compared to intercritical ferrite, verifying the dissolution of martensite into reverted austenite and intercritical ferrite during annealing. No precipitates or elemental clustering are observed, ruling out carbide contribution to the specimen's excellent product of strength and elongation, which is attributed to the enhanced TRIP effect instead.
